chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Why has working become a mundane part of our lives rather than something full of passion and joy? One reason might be capitalism itself.

In capitalism, even if labor doesn't always result in pleasure, there is no pleasure without work.

Capitalism is an economic system where private owners control commerce and industry for profit. This structure often allows a small group of wealthy individuals to dominate economic activities, frequently at the expense of the broader workforce.

In this system, not everyone can find suitable work, and societal barriers often prevent certain groups from gaining employment. Moreover, the job market is increasingly competitive, leading organizations to exploit job seekers' desperation by offering low wages and unfair conditions. This exploitation strengthens corporate power, polarizing the workforce and diminishing middle-class job opportunities.

Work in capitalism is often unrewarding, yet it remains necessary for any semblance of pleasure in life. The high degree of competitiveness at work makes it hard to leave unsatisfactory jobs, intertwining our happiness with our professional lives.

Further, there are more insights into the interplay between work and life and the adverse impacts of capitalism on both. Curious? Then follow along!

chapters.title: Change capitalism by understanding it
chapters.text:
For years, political analysts have debated the meaning of capitalism, but many standard analyses have been superficial.

While viewed as beneficial to all, capitalism often forces those in power and corporations to behave in damaging ways, with little oversight by the public. It usually justifies questionable individual decisions, such as purchasing overpriced or unethically manufactured items, or corporate decisions prioritizing profits over social responsibility.

To reform capitalism, we must understand its fundamental dynamics, how it operates, and its individual and collective impacts. We must also explain how and why it can be harmful and devise and fight for viable alternatives.

Due to limited government intervention, capitalism can result in inequity, market failure, and environmental damage.

One way to examine capitalism is through work. The labor conditions under capitalism are not situational but a fundamental component of systemic oppression. For instance, women currently make up the majority of garment workers, yet only about 2% earn a livable wage based on local housing, food, education, and childcare costs.

Sadly, the working circumstances of garment workers have barely changed from the late 1800s to today. There are ongoing reports of building collapses, fires, sexual harassment, and the physical and emotional toll of arduous labor.

Capitalist ideology advocates that workers are free to contract with employers and sell their skills to the highest bidder. However, given the current global economic reality, few can exist without 'freely' entering such contracts, making this freedom questionable. Moreover, workers rarely have the power to modify the terms offered by organizations.

Essentially, capitalism relies on workers who are disconnected from production profits, utilizing both unfree and allegedly free labor. Despite the importance of contract-bound employment to capitalism, more people today work for meager wages than ever before. Contrary to popular belief, capitalism often doesn’t work for the masses; it works against them.

chapters.title: It’s hard to stay human under capitalism
chapters.text:
Employment is a broad term used to describe any paid job or workplace. Depending on the conditions, the same activity you engage in might be both work and non-work. For example, cleaning someone else’s home for a wage is labor, but cleaning your house isn’t. While many believe that leisure and work are mutually incompatible, others argue that capitalism has blurred those lines.

Greed always triumphs over democracy in a society passively controlled by capitalists and elites.

Under capitalism, workers are free to sell their labor. Yet this raises a problem — who will take care of their fundamental needs so they can work? For instance, buying groceries, paying for services, or purchasing real estate to start a family.

However, some of these requests aren't fully met by the market. Additionally, activities seemingly unrelated to capitalism, such as unpaid housework, actively support the system by providing essential services without compensation. Many organizations also expect their employees to be available outside regular work hours, often without additional pay.

…Under capitalism, work takes something human and turns it into something monstrous. ~ Amelia Horgan

Likewise, service workers like air hostesses must maintain a specific demeanor regardless of whether their relative recently passed away or the plane is about to crash. Naturally, this emotional labor is mentally taxing. These employees must appear emotionally stable for passengers, following scripts without complaint or deviation from the norm. Furthermore, there's a famous saying that "the customer is always right," which leaves employees vulnerable to demanding or abusive customers.

In short, traditional customer service skills alienate people from their jobs and themselves. It is one of the main reasons for high rates of depression and dissatisfaction among today's workforce.

chapters.title: Control makes us happy
chapters.text:
Under capitalism, work satisfaction is not spread evenly. Some people have the privilege of combining their jobs with passion, while others sacrifice their time just to make a living. Although work can provide meaning and recognition, we spend so much time there that finding joy or purpose elsewhere becomes challenging.

For example, jobs involving toxic chemicals may be detrimental to health. Some jobs are unpleasant, difficult, or inconvenient, yet they must be done. These are more extreme cases than others, but only to an extent. Our work significantly influences our health and relationships with ourselves and others. Workplace stress can cause both psychological and physical problems, which are tightly linked and shouldn’t be treated separately.

Employees often push themselves to extremes at work, staying longer or working faster to appear more dedicated and resilient. While this may seem like a personal choice and even feel empowering, it's often a response to underlying pressures. Many employees feel obligated to demonstrate intense commitment to their jobs, which creates more stress than satisfaction. These behaviors are primarily a result of capitalism's systemic influence on work culture.

In modern society, simply getting the job done is not enough. In other words, performing well, mastering skills, and being content within the role may be seen as underperforming. Employers expect workers to improve continually, treat everyone as customers, update their methods, and change work processes, all for compensation that often doesn't reflect the value of their work or efforts.

We have little control over our jobs, so facing the problems we encounter at work is hard. Addressing workplace difficulties such as a lack of critical safety equipment, unpaid overtime, harassment, and discrimination is becoming more challenging.

When we depend on a job for survival, our employers exert greater control over us.

So, while labor may be dangerous, exploitative, or merely repetitive, all work under capitalism harms people due to pressure and a lack of control.

chapters.title: Corporate trust issues
chapters.text:
Capitalism supports private ownership of commerce and industry for profit and is often closely linked with state powers. This structure allows a few wealthy individuals to dominate economic activities, frequently sidelining the broader workforce.

In this system, employment is crucial but flawed. Only some people can find suitable work, and societal barriers often prevent certain groups from gaining employment. Despite advances in science and technology eliminating many hazardous jobs, many workers still face poor health conditions, exploitation, and authoritarian employers, perpetuating a cycle enabled by capitalist interests.

The increasingly competitive job market encourages organizations to exploit job seekers' desperation by offering low wages and unfair conditions. This exploitation strengthens corporate power and flexibility, further dividing the workforce and reducing middle-class job opportunities. As a result, economic power becomes more concentrated in the hands of a few elites.

Work in capitalism is often unrewarding, yet it remains necessary for enjoyment. Due to high competition in the workplace, it is increasingly difficult to leave unsatisfactory jobs, tying our happiness to our professional lives. This discussion highlights how work and life are connected and how capitalism negatively affects both.

Today, work goes beyond a daily routine — it has become a process of capitalist exploitation and a cornerstone of capitalist institutions. Employers foster distrust through surveillance measures like spy cameras, showing the mutual suspicion inherent in capitalism's unequal labor relations.

While capitalism has created unprecedented riches, only some have equal access to this abundance.

Legal frameworks often favor private companies, providing minimal protection for employees. For instance, organizations can terminate employment immediately, while employees usually must provide extensive notice before quitting. This imbalance reflects the broader inequalities of capitalism, where not everyone can access the abundance created. If governments don’t curb the powers of private entities, workplace issues will persist, necessitating stronger protections for workers against exploitation.

Did you know? While productivity in the US grew by about 80% from 1973 to 2020, wages only increased by about 9%, illustrating the stark "productivity-pay gap."

chapters.title: Crossing the bridge between poor and rich
chapters.text:
Capitalist organizations want to keep people engaged for as long as possible, which takes many forms. For example, employers lure employees to work longer hours by offering free pizza and drinks or promising extra benefits that hold little to no value. As workers, we are subtly but constantly reminded that our time at work is not our own; it belongs to the company, and they will do everything in their power to retain it.

Poverty and unemployment become individual failings rather than unwanted features of the economy. ~ Amelia Horgan

Due to power play, situations like unpaid wages, abusive employers, and racial or sexist harassment are rarely dealt with. It’s difficult to get justice against those protected by influential people. Without mass coordination of our grievances, the best we can hope for outside of social reform is that our next job will be marginally less horrible than the one we left or currently hold.

At times, we might reach a breaking point. It's when the harsh demands from managers, petty punishments for minor violations like fatigue, and the sudden realization that our hard work primarily benefits the company, not ourselves, finally hit us. When these moments happen, we must deal with them and move on. Survival often becomes our primary motivation, as there seems to be no reprieve.

Most individuals have little control over their employment situation, and employees have limited societal authority.

Sure, the obvious solution would be to either work less or stop working entirely; many people have tried to do so. Labor resistance may improve life for those who manage it, but it won’t result in a more equitable outcome for everyone.

Ultimately, trade unions should turn a grumble into a political issue and then into collective action at work. Although a union-coordinated effort is a risky short-term gamble, it can potentially change the direction of personal and communal life. Remember, if we fail to take risks, capitalism will continue to expand, and only the wealthy will benefit.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
While capitalism has driven efficiency and growth, it has also faced criticism for prioritizing profit over workers' needs, often limiting genuine freedom in the labor market. This system allows the rich to get richer while the average worker faces stagnating wages and deteriorating work conditions — the stark reality of the "productivity-pay gap."

Moving forward, meaningful change is necessary to rebalance this power dynamic. Strengthening worker protections and revitalizing the trade union movement could ensure that employment offers more than mere survival. The goal should be a system where individuals are valued as much as profits, allowing workers to thrive.

Try this

• Advocate for your workplace rights. Know and discuss your rights with coworkers to foster a supportive work environment.
• Support or join a union. Collective action can lead to better wages and conditions.
• Seek balance. Prioritize your health and wellbeing by setting boundaries at work to prevent burnout.
